title: Beer on Cotton
timeEstimate: 10–20 minutes (fresh); aged beer adds a light-brown ring
stainChemistry: Beer is a low-tannin, low-sugar, low-protein stain — malt tannins (polyphenols) + hop resins + barley proteins + dissolved sugars. The stain is light and often invisible on dark cotton while fresh; it's the DRIED version that shows up as a faint tan / yellow-brown ring after the sugar caramelizes and the tannin oxidizes. Cotton is pure cellulose — tolerates NSD, acid-side spotters, 3% H₂O₂, and hot post-chemistry wash.
whyThisWorks: Beer is one of the easier tannin stains because the pigment load is low. NSD dissolves the sugar and surface residue in one pass. For the residual ring — usually only visible on white or light fabric — an acid-side rinse or peroxide clears the oxidized tannin. Hot wash finishes.
defaultAssumption: Fresh beer on cotton rinses out almost fully with water. Aged or dried beer is the harder version — faint ring that shows up only after drying. Expectation: fresh = trivial; aged = one chemistry pass.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric. Blot from underneath. Most fresh beer lifts here because the pigment is light and water-soluble.
Apply NSD, sponge gently. NSD handles the sugar residue + residual barley protein + any remaining pigment.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — acid-side tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: For aged beer where a faint ring remains, acid-side tannin spotter by dropper. Dwell 2–3 min, blot. Fresh beer rarely needs this step.
Optional: 3% H₂O₂ on aged oxidized rings on white cotton. One oxidizer at a time. Skip on dark cotton.
Rinse. Launder hot per care label.

steps: 1. Cold flush from the back — fresh beer mostly lifts here.
2. NSD in cool water. Sponge, 2–3 min.
3. Aged ring only: acid-side tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter / PRENETT-A). 2–3 min.
4. Aged white: 3% H₂O₂ on residual oxidized ring.
products: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — aged only
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Beer's low pigment means most fresh spills are a 2-step job — don't over-treat
Aged beer stains oxidize to a light brown; inspect dried fabric to confirm residual color

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Flush Is Often Enough for Fresh
homeSolutions.instruction: Beer is a light stain compared to wine or coffee. Fresh beer usually rinses right out of cotton with cold water alone — hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water for 30 seconds and you'll often be done. The tough variant is BEER THAT DRIED overnight, which oxidizes into a faint tan ring.
For anything that didn't rinse out: a drop of Dawn dish soap worked into the spot with a fingertip for a minute. Rinse cool. This handles the sugar and barley residue the water left behind.

homeSolutions.agent: White Vinegar Dab for Aged Ring
homeSolutions.instruction: If a faint tan ring remains from beer that sat overnight, mix 1 teaspoon white vinegar with 3 tablespoons cool water. Dab on the ring, let sit 2–3 minutes, blot. The mild acidity breaks the oxidized tannin.
For a stubborn aged ring on WHITE cotton: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide, let it bubble 3 minutes, blot. Skip on dark or dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia — toxic fume risk.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. Fresh beer that's been flushed is usually fully clear before wash; aged rings may need one more soap + vinegar pass.

Beer rarely needs a pro. If the stain has been ironed or gone through a hot dryer AND you can still see it after home treatment, take to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Beer on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + vinegar + 3% peroxide on whites."

steps: 1. Cold water flush from the BACK — often enough by itself for fresh beer.
2. Dawn + cool water for residual sugar / protein.
3. Diluted white vinegar dab for aged tan ring.
4. 3% hydrogen peroxide on stubborn oxidized ring (whites only).
5. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
6. Escalate only if heat-set + home round fails.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if beer has been iron-set or hot-dried AND home round didn't clear the residue. Take to a dry cleaner. But most beer on cotton clears without ever needing a pro.
warnings: Cold water first — fresh beer rinses out easily
Hot water on aged stains sets the oxidized tannin
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia

neverDo: Never hot-water-flush aged beer — sets the tan ring
Never over-treat fresh beer — usually one step gets it out
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates NSD, acid-side spotters, 3% H₂O₂, hot wash
Dyed cotton: skip or inside-seam-test peroxide

materialWarnings: Fresh beer is easy; aged beer leaves a tan ring
Don't over-treat — often one rinse suffices

escalation.when: Beer heat-set by iron or dryer AND home round didn't clear residual. This is rare.
escalation.whatToTell: "Beer on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + vinegar + [3% H₂O₂ on whites]."

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — fresh or overnight+?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Regular beer or stout/dark? (dark beers have more tannin)
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Beer is the lightest-pigment common tannin stain. Fresh = usually just water. Aged is the real stain. Dark / stout beers raise the tannin load a notch but stay in the same protocol.
